title: Money Conversations With a Spouse Who Won't Look
dek: When the spreadsheet opens and they look away, your body registers the abandonment before your brain registers the numbers. Here is how to stay in your own skin when the person you love refuses to see the financial reality, and how to metabolize the fury that rises in your jaw.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Spreadsheet and the Psoas


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.236Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You are sitting at the kitchen table. The laptop is open. The blue light of the screen casts a pale, sickly glow across the wood grain. It is 9:43 PM on a Tuesday, the air outside heavy with the damp smell of incoming rain. You have spent the last three hours organizing the numbers, tracking the outflows, looking at the reality of the bank accounts. You have done the work. You have gathered the evidence. You have prepared yourself for this conversation.

And then, your spouse sits down. They do not look at the screen. They look at their phone. They look at the wall. They look anywhere but at the numbers. They say, "It will be fine. We always figure it out." 

Notice what happens in your body in this exact moment. It is not a metaphor. It is a biological event. The masseter muscles in your jaw clench so tightly your teeth ache. Your breath, which was moving smoothly through your diaphragm, suddenly stops, trapped high in your chest. The psoas muscles - the deep core muscles that connect your spine to your legs, the muscles of fight or flight - contract violently. Your body is preparing to run, or to strike. 

This is not a moral failure on your part. This is not you being "unspiritual" or "unhealed." This is your nervous system recognizing a profound threat. When the person you share your life with refuses to look at the reality of your shared survival, your body registers it as abandonment. You are alone in the watchtower, and the other guard has gone to sleep.

Bessel van der Kolk, in his foundational work on trauma, reminds us that the body keeps the score. The body does not care about your spouse's intentions. The body does not care that they are overwhelmed or anxious. The body only knows that the numbers are real, the threat is real, and you are holding the weight of it alone. The fury that rises in you is not a character flaw. It is a messenger. It is your biology screaming that something is out of balance, that your safety is compromised.

## The Anatomy of Financial Avoidance

When they look away, they are not just avoiding a spreadsheet. They are avoiding the somatic reality of limitation. Money is not just math; it is survival energy. It is the roof, the food, the heat in the winter. When they refuse to look, they are outsourcing the burden of survival to you. 

You feel it in the levator scapulae, the muscles that run down the sides of your neck. They tighten, pulling your shoulders up toward your ears, bracing for impact. You are carrying the cognitive load, the emotional load, and the somatic load. 

Gabor Maté teaches us that much of our behavior is driven by an unconscious attempt to avoid pain. Your spouse's refusal to look is a defense mechanism. It is a dissociation from the terror of scarcity. But understanding their trauma does not negate your reality. You can have deep compassion for their fear while simultaneously honoring the absolute necessity of your own anger. 

Your anger is the boundary. It is the fire that says, "I will not carry this alone." Karla McLaren, a pioneer in the study of emotions, describes anger as the honorable sentry. It tells you what needs to be protected and what needs to be restored. When your spouse looks away, your anger is the sentry sounding the alarm. It is not something to be managed, suppressed, or meditated away. It is something to be listened to.

## The Herbal Support for the Sentry

When the fire of anger burns too hot, it can consume you. In Traditional Chinese Medicine, anger is associated with the Liver meridian. When the Liver qi is stagnant, the anger turns inward, creating depression, or explodes outward, creating destruction. We do not want to suppress the fire, but we want to give it a channel to flow through.

This is where the plants come in. They are not a cure for a spouse who won't look at the budget. They are allies for a nervous system that is carrying too much weight. 

Consider [Pure Encapsulations Daily Calm](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DLCX6T4K?tag=spankyspinola-20). Chamomile is often dismissed as a gentle bedtime tea, but it is a profound nervine. It specifically targets the tension in the solar plexus, the exact place where the stomach knots when the financial conversation goes sideways. It relaxes the smooth muscle tissue, allowing the breath to drop back down into the belly. 

When the jaw is tight and the mind is racing with calculations at 3:00 AM, [FES Yarrow Environmental Solution Spray (2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00F3SKA7M?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a fierce ally. Skullcap is for the nervous system that is exhausted but cannot stop working. It is for the mind that is spinning like a hamster wheel, trying to solve a two-person problem with one person's energy. It does not numb you; it simply turns down the volume of the panic so you can actually hear your own intuition.

## The Somatic Practice of Staying

When they look away, the temptation is to either explode or collapse. To yell, or to shut the laptop and say, "Fine, I'll just do it myself." Both are trauma responses. Both are ways of leaving your own body.

The practice is to stay. 

**The Practice: Grounding the Sentry**

1. When you feel the jaw clench and the breath stop, do not immediately speak. 
2. Press your feet into the floor. Feel the actual physical sensation of the ground beneath you. The ground is holding you, even if your spouse is not.
3. Notice the tension in your body. Name it silently. "My jaw is tight. My chest is restricted."
4. Take one breath that reaches all the way down to your pelvic floor. 
5. Look at your spouse. Do not look at the spreadsheet. Look at them. 
6. Speak from the sensation, not the story. Instead of saying, "You never care about this," say, "When you look away from the screen, my chest gets tight because I feel like I am holding this alone."

This is not a magic trick. It will not instantly make them look at the numbers. But it keeps you in your body. It keeps you from abandoning yourself in the face of their abandonment.

## The Illusion of the High Vibration

There is a pervasive myth in modern spiritual circles that if you just "raise your vibration" or "focus on abundance," the money will sort itself out. This is a dangerous dissociation. It is a bypass of the physical reality of living in a capitalist system. 

When your spouse uses spiritual language to avoid financial reality - saying things like, "The universe will provide," or "I just don't want to focus on lack" - notice what happens in your gut. The nausea that arises is your body's bullshit detector. 

Resmaa Menakem writes profoundly about how trauma is stored in the body, and how we must metabolize it somatically, not just cognitively. Financial trauma is real trauma. The fear of not having enough to survive is a primal terror. When we try to bypass that terror with "love and light," we are abandoning the parts of ourselves that are genuinely terrified. 

Your anger at their spiritual bypass is holy. It is the part of you that insists on living in reality. It is the part of you that knows that the universe might provide, but the universe does not pay the electric bill. You do. 

## The Boundary of the Shared Reality

You cannot force another person to look at what they are terrified to see. You cannot drag them into reality. But you can refuse to participate in their delusion. 

This requires a profound level of somatic boundaries. A boundary is not a wall you build to keep them out; it is a clear delineation of where you end and they begin. 

When they refuse to look, you must decide what you will and will not carry. You might say, "I will manage the daily budget, but I will not protect you from the consequences of overspending." You might say, "I will not have this conversation while you are looking at your phone." 

Setting these boundaries will feel terrifying in the body. The heart will race. The palms will sweat. This is normal. You are changing the choreography of the relationship. You are stepping out of the dance of over-functioning and under-functioning. 

Pema Chödrön teaches about the practice of staying with the groundlessness of the present moment. When you set a boundary, you step into groundlessness. You do not know how they will react. You do not know if the relationship can survive the truth. But the alternative is to slowly suffocate under the weight of the unspoken reality.

## The Medicine of the Bitter Truth

Sometimes, the medicine we need is bitter. In herbalism, bitter herbs stimulate digestion. They wake up the liver, they get the bile flowing, they force the body to process what it has taken in. 

The truth of your financial situation might be bitter. The truth of your spouse's avoidance might be bitter. But swallowing the bitter truth is the only way to digest it. 

If you need support in digesting the bitter truth, [Solaray Total Calm Advanced](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z1I5EC?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be a grounding force. Ashwagandha is an adaptogen; it helps the body adapt to stress over time. It is not a quick fix, but a slow, steady building of resilience. It supports the adrenal glands, which are often depleted when we are carrying the chronic stress of financial instability and relational disconnect.

## The Long Game of Financial Somatics

This is not a conversation you will have once. This is a practice. It is the practice of returning to your body, over and over again, when the numbers are scary and the partner is absent. 

It is the practice of feeling the texture of the wood table under your hands. It is the practice of smelling the rain outside the window. It is the practice of knowing that you are here, you are breathing, and you are capable of facing the reality of your life.

You do not have to be calm. You do not have to be detached. You can be furious. You can be terrified. But you must be present. 

Your body is the only home you have that cannot be foreclosed on. Do not abandon it just because they have abandoned the spreadsheet. Stay in the house of your own skin. Keep the fire burning in the hearth. Let the sentry stand guard. You are worth the effort of your own presence.

## FAQ

**Q: What if my spouse gets angry when I try to talk about money?**
**A:** Notice their anger, and notice your body's response to it. Often, their anger is a secondary emotion covering their deep shame or fear about money. You do not have to absorb their anger. You can say, "I see that this is activating for you. We can pause, but we cannot avoid this forever." Keep your feet on the floor. Do not match their escalation, but do not shrink away from it either.

**Q: I feel like I'm being controlling by insisting we look at the budget. Am I?**
**A:** There is a difference between controlling another person and insisting on a shared reality. Wanting to know how much money is in the bank is not controlling; it is basic survival. If your spouse accuses you of being controlling, recognize that this is often a deflection tactic. Stay grounded in your own somatic truth: you need to know the numbers to feel safe in your body.

**Q: How do I stop resenting them for making me do all the financial work?**
**A:** You don't stop resenting them by pretending you don't resent them. You stop resenting them by honoring the resentment as a valid signal that the arrangement is unfair. Resentment is just anger that has been swallowed and left to ferment. Bring it up. Speak it out loud. "I am carrying the entire cognitive load of our finances, and my body is exhausted by it." Let the resentment transform back into clean, clear anger, and use that anger to set a new boundary.

**Q: What if the financial reality is actually really bad, and I'm terrified too?**
**A:** Then you start by acknowledging the terror. Do not try to positive-think your way out of it. Feel the fear in your belly. Cry. Shake. Let the somatic energy of the terror move through you. Once the energy has moved, you will have more access to your prefrontal cortex, the part of your brain that can actually solve the problem. You can face a terrible reality, but only if you are fully in your body to do it.

## The Weight of the Unspoken

There is a specific gravity to the things we do not say. It settles in the fascia, the connective tissue that wraps around every muscle and organ in your body. When you swallow the words "we cannot afford this," they do not disappear. They lodge in the throat. They tighten the vocal cords. They create a chronic, low-grade constriction that you carry with you to the grocery store, to the gas station, to the bed you share with the person who will not look.

Thich Nhat Hanh taught that understanding is the other name of love. If you do not understand the reality of your shared life, how can you truly love each other? When your spouse refuses to understand the financial reality, they are, in a very real somatic sense, refusing to love the part of you that is carrying the burden. They are loving a fantasy of you, a fantasy where you are the endless provider, the magic money tree, the mother who always makes it work. 

You are not a magic money tree. You are a human animal with a nervous system that requires safety to function. 

Notice the smell of the room when the conversation shuts down. Is it stale? Does the air feel thick? Your olfactory senses are directly wired to the amygdala, the fear center of the brain. The smell of avoidance becomes a trigger. Years from now, you might smell that specific combination of damp rain and old coffee, and your stomach will instantly knot, anticipating the abandonment. This is how deeply the body records the relational dynamic.

## The False Peace of Accommodation

For a long time, you probably accommodated their avoidance. You did the math in secret. You moved money from savings to checking before they could notice the overdraft. You canceled your own dental appointment so they could buy the thing they "really needed." 

You told yourself you were keeping the peace. But there is no peace in the body when the mind is constantly calculating. 

Look at your posture right now. Are your shoulders rounded forward, protecting your heart? Is your breath shallow? Accommodation is a physical posture of shrinking. It is the body making itself small so the other person's delusion can take up all the space in the room. 

Robert Augustus Masters writes about the difference between blind compassion and fierce compassion. Blind compassion avoids conflict at all costs. It enables the dysfunction because it cannot tolerate the discomfort of the truth. Fierce compassion, on the other hand, is willing to burn down the false peace to build something real. It is the mother bear protecting her cubs. It is the anger that says, "No more."

When you stop accommodating, the system will go into shock. Your spouse will likely escalate their avoidance or turn it into blame. "Why are you suddenly making such a big deal out of this?" they will ask. 

Your body will want to retreat. The adrenaline will spike, making your hands shake. The urge to apologize, to smooth it over, to say "never mind, I'll handle it," will be overwhelming. 

This is the crucible. This is where the actual work happens. 

## The Somatic Anchor in the Storm

You must find an anchor in your body when the storm of their reaction hits. You cannot rely on them to regulate your nervous system. You must become your own anchor.

Feel the weight of your pelvis in the chair. The pelvis is the bowl that holds your vital organs. It is the center of gravity. When we are in fight or flight, our energy moves up into the chest and head. We become top-heavy, easily knocked over by a harsh word or a dismissive sigh. 

Send your awareness down into the bowl of your pelvis. Imagine it heavy, filled with warm water or wet sand. Let the breath drop down into that bowl. 

This is not about calming down. You do not need to be calm. You need to be rooted. A tree in a hurricane is not calm, but if its roots are deep enough, it does not blow over. 

## The Herbal Allies for the Deep Roots

When you are doing the grueling work of holding a boundary against someone else's delusion, your nervous system burns through minerals at an astonishing rate. You are literally depleting your physical reserves to maintain your psychological stance.

This is why we turn to the deep, nourishing herbs. We are not looking for a quick fix; we are looking for foundational support. 

Nettle leaf is the supreme ally here. It is a dark, earthy green, tasting of the soil itself. It is packed with calcium, magnesium, and iron - the exact minerals the nervous system needs to rebuild the myelin sheath around the nerves. When you drink a strong infusion of nettle, you are drinking the earth's resilience. You are giving your body the raw materials it needs to stand its ground.

Oatstraw is another profound restorative. It is the dried stem of the oat plant, and it specifically feeds the nervous system. It is for the person who is "fried," whose nerves feel like exposed wires. It does not sedate; it rebuilds. It is the slow, steady accumulation of strength. 

When you prepare these herbs, do not rush. Make it a somatic ritual. Feel the heat of the water. Smell the earthy, grassy scent of the plants. Notice the dark green color of the infusion. As you drink, visualize the minerals traveling to the exact places in your body that feel weak or shaky. You are fortifying the walls of your own house.

## The Grief of the Unmet Need

Beneath the anger, beneath the frustration of the spreadsheet, there is a profound grief. 

It is the grief of realizing that the person you chose to partner with cannot, or will not, meet you in the reality of your shared life. It is the grief of the unmet need for safety, for partnership, for someone to say, "I see how hard this is, and I am here with you."

Do not bypass this grief. Do not let the anger harden into a shell that prevents you from feeling the sorrow. 

Grief has a different somatic signature than anger. Anger is hot, upward-moving, and expansive. Grief is heavy, downward-moving, and contracting. It feels like a physical weight on the sternum. It feels like an ache in the back of the throat. 

Let the grief have its time. Let yourself cry for the partnership you want but do not currently have. Crying is a biological release valve. It flushes cortisol out of the body through the tears. It softens the hardened fascia. 

When you allow the grief to move through you, the anger becomes cleaner. It is no longer mixed with the desperate hope that they will change. It becomes a clear, cold fact: "This is the reality. They will not look. Therefore, I must decide what I will do."

## The Choice of Reality

You are at a crossroads. You can continue to live in the false peace of accommodation, carrying the somatic load until your body breaks down in illness or exhaustion. Or you can choose reality.

Choosing reality means accepting that they might never look at the spreadsheet. It means accepting that you cannot control their avoidance. 

But it also means reclaiming your own energy. When you stop trying to force them to look, you free up a massive amount of somatic bandwidth. You can use that energy to take care of yourself. You can use it to make financial decisions that protect your own future. You can use it to build a life that feels safe in your own body, regardless of what they do.

This is the ultimate somatic boundary. "I will live in reality, even if I have to live there alone."

It is a lonely place at first. The air is thinner. The responsibility is heavy. But it is real. And the body, above all else, craves what is real. The body knows the difference between a comforting lie and a difficult truth. 

When you finally align with the truth, you will notice a subtle shift in your physiology. The jaw might still be tight, the shoulders might still ache, but there will be a new solidity in your core. The psoas will stop bracing for the next disappointment, because you have stopped expecting them to be someone they are not. 

You have become your own sentry. You have become your own anchor. You have looked at the numbers, you have felt the terror, and you have not abandoned yourself. 

That is the true wealth. That is the safety that cannot be taken away.

Do not bypass this grief. Do not let the anger harden into a shell that prevents you from feeling the sorrow. 

Grief has a different somatic signature than anger. Anger is hot, upward-moving, and expansive. Grief is heavy, downward-moving, and contracting. It feels like a physical weight on the sternum. It feels like an ache in the back of the throat. 

Let the grief have its time. Let yourself cry for the partnership you want but do not currently have. Crying is a biological release valve. It flushes cortisol out of the body through the tears. It softens the hardened fascia. 

When you allow the grief to move through you, the anger becomes cleaner. It is no longer mixed with the desperate hope that they will change. It becomes a clear, cold fact: "This is the reality. They will not look. Therefore, I must decide what I will do."

## The Choice of Reality

You are at a crossroads. You can continue to live in the false peace of accommodation, carrying the somatic load until your body breaks down in illness or exhaustion. Or you can choose reality.

Choosing reality means accepting that they might never look at the spreadsheet. It means accepting that you cannot control their avoidance. 

But it also means reclaiming your own energy. When you stop trying to force them to look, you free up a massive amount of somatic bandwidth. You can use that energy to take care of yourself. You can use it to make financial decisions that protect your own future. You can use it to build a life that feels safe in your own body, regardless of what they do.

This is the ultimate somatic boundary. "I will live in reality, even if I have to live there alone."

It is a lonely place at first. The air is thinner. The responsibility is heavy. But it is real. And the body, above all else, craves what is real. The body knows the difference between a comforting lie and a difficult truth. 

When you finally align with the truth, you will notice a subtle shift in your physiology. The jaw might still be tight, the shoulders might still ache, but there will be a new solidity in your core. The psoas will stop bracing for the next disappointment, because you have stopped expecting them to be someone they are not. 

You have become your own sentry. You have become your own anchor. You have looked at the numbers, you have felt the terror, and you have not abandoned yourself. 

That is the true wealth. That is the safety that cannot be taken away.

---

<section data-author="byline">
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality.
